framework: Claude for Financial Services — equity-research methodology (/screen, /sector, /comps, /catalysts, /thesis). Data: Robinhood fundamentals + web_search (no paid-vendor MCP connectors).
action: Rotate the defensive/value sleeve into a semis-led leadership book: exit JPM and GE, cut UNH, keep BAC as the lone cyclical diversifier, add MRVL/TSM and a LLY growth hedge; lift deployment to ~62% with cash as the only downside hedge.
sizing: 5 names, ~62% invested / 38% cash. MU 0.16, MRVL 0.15, TSM 0.13, LLY 0.10, BAC 0.08. Semis ~44% of book (MU/MRVL/TSM), so cash is the explicit hedge against a sector-correlated drawdown. All orders market-on-open (no limits) — the prior-tick lesson was under-participating in leadership, so prioritize fills over a cuter entry.

regime.label: Risk-on; Fed on hold with cuts priced; AI-semis leadership; energy de-escalating
regime.note: Quarter is closing as the best for the S&P 500/Nasdaq in six years. Fed held at 3.50-3.75% with the market pricing two 2026 cuts. The Iran war is winding down (peace talks set to resume 6/30), pulling oil lower and relieving cyclicals on growth hopes; AI semis are the clear leadership (6/29 Nasdaq-100 +2.3%). Tilt: own the semis leaders with room on the RSI, keep one cheap financial for the de-escalation rotation, hold a non-cyclical growth hedge (LLY), and let cash — not a defensive stock — be the downside hedge.

thesis: What changed since 6/29: the risk-on inflection I flagged last tick is now confirmed by the tape, so I'm committing capital to it instead of starting it. The energy/Iran shock is de-escalating (oil falling, peace talks resuming), the Fed is on hold with two cuts priced, and AI semiconductors are unambiguous market leadership (6/29 Nasdaq-100 +2.3%; Astera +16%, KLA +12%, AMAT +11%). My relative-strength screen across the universe puts semis at the top (MRVL +35.7% / MU +11.2% / TSM +9.6% over 20d vs SPY -1.6%) while energy is the worst sleeve (SLB -15%, OXY -18%, RSI 13-25) — the same rotation. So I rotate the financials/industrials value sleeve (exit JPM, exit GE, cut the failed UNH hedge) into a semis-led book anchored on the best risk/reward, not the most extended: MU (cheapest semis P/E at 25.9, memory/HBM super-cycle, pulled back -8% from its high), MRVL (the custom-AI-silicon leader, but RSI only 57 with a -10% pullback = room), and TSM (foundry monopoly at a reasonable 37.8x into 7/16 earnings). LLY adds non-cyclical GLP-1 growth that doesn't move with semis, and BAC stays as one cheap (14.4x) financial that benefits from the de-escalation/growth rotation into its 7/14 earnings. I keep 38% cash because the book is semis-concentrated and I'm down 10.6% all-time — cash, not a bleeding defensive name, is the hedge.

evidence.point: S&P 500 is closing 'the best quarter in years'; on 6/29 the S&P rose 1.2%, the Nasdaq-100 surged 2.3%, and the Dow gained 307 pts to a fresh record high.
Fed held at 3.50%-3.75% with investors continuing to price in two cuts for 2026.
'The potential wind down of the Iran war' with 'falling oil prices helping relieve pressure on more cyclical sectors like industrials and financials'; Trump says peace talks resume Tuesday.
Semis led: the VanEck Semiconductor ETF gained more than 3%, with Astera Labs ~+16%, KLA ~+12%, and Applied Materials ~+11% (6/29).
'Micron's rally has room to run as the AI semiconductor cycle continues to reshape the memory business'; MU rose 10% on 6/15 on Iran de-escalation + AI chip-demand reports.
MU P/E 25.93, 52-wk high 1255.00 (6/25), last 1151 (-8.3% from high), RSI 61 — cheapest semis multiple in the finalist set.

Marvell 'has significantly benefited from large cloud companies designing custom AI accelerators, particularly in networking and optical interconnect.' MRVL P/E 94.9, 52-wk high 329.88 (6/18), today ranged 275.50-300.00, RSI 57, +35.7% over 20d.

'Taiwan Semiconductor said the demand for AI chips is growing significantly, with customers giving a very positive outlook.' TSM P/E 37.8, set a 52-wk high of 479.00 on 6/30; Q2 earnings 2026-07-16, est EPS 3.77.
LLY P/E 44.3, 52-wk high 1238.00 (6/29), RSI 61 — non-cyclical GLP-1 growth uncorrelated to semis.
BAC P/E 14.36, dividend yield 1.9%, RSI 68; Q2 earnings 2026-07-14 (am), est EPS 1.10 — cheap financial leveraged to the de-escalation/growth rotation.

risks: Semis concentration (~44% of book in MU/MRVL/TSM): a sector-wide selloff like 6/4-6/5 (AMD/INTC down hard, MU -7%) hits all three together; cash (38%) and the 0.20 per-name stop are the only ballast.
Chasing extended leadership: TSM (52-wk high), and the group has already run a full quarter — a momentum reversal or profit-taking into quarter-end/start could whipsaw fresh entries.
TSM 7/16 earnings is binary: a soft AI/foundry print or weak guide would re-rate the whole semis sleeve, not just TSM.
Re-escalation risk: if Iran peace talks collapse, oil spikes back, cyclicals/risk reverse, and the de-escalation thesis underpinning the rotation breaks.
ASML-style equipment-order worries (semicap fell 5-6% on order concerns earlier in June) could spread to the broader semis complex.

data_examined.label: Universe relative-strength / momentum / RSI screen across all 100 names off the 2026-06-30 snapshot (SPY 20d -1.6%)
Robinhood fundamentals (P/E, 52-wk range, market cap, dividend) on 10 finalists: MRVL/ASML/AMD/MU/TSM/INTC/LLY/GE/BAC/CAT
Robinhood earnings calendar, next 31 days, high-cap: BAC 7/14, ASML 7/15, TSM 7/16, GE 7/16, INTC 7/23
Macro regime + semis demand read (Fed path, Iran de-escalation, oil, semis leadership, Deloitte $975B 2026 chip-sales outlook)

rationale.MU: Anchor and highest conviction. [DATA] Cheapest semis multiple in the set (P/E 25.9 vs MRVL 95 / AMD 177), riding the AI memory/HBM super-cycle that analysts say 'has room to run.' [DATA] It pulled back -8.3% from its 6/25 52-wk high (1255) to ~1151 with RSI at 61 — a better entry than the extended leaders. [ASSUMED] I already hold it (+1.5% vs entry) and not panic-selling the 6/26 -7.5% scare was validated; I'm bumping it to the top weight. Catalyst: continued AI-memory demand reports; ex-dividend 7/6.
rationale.MRVL: The cleanest leadership risk/reward. [DATA] Strongest 20-day relative strength in the universe (+35.7% vs SPY -1.6%) yet RSI is only 57 and it sits -9.7% below its 6/18 high (329.88) — i.e. a pullback in the leader, not a blow-off top; today it reclaimed $300 after dipping to 275.50. [DATA] Structurally it's the custom-AI-silicon / optical-interconnect winner as hyperscalers design their own accelerators. [ASSUMED] New position — the highest-momentum name I can still enter without chasing an overbought RSI. Next earnings >31 days out, so this is a thematic AI-capex call, not an earnings bet.
rationale.TSM: Quality foundry anchor. [DATA] Monopoly on leading-edge nodes at a reasonable 37.8x; management says AI-chip demand is 'growing significantly' with a 'very positive' customer outlook; printed a 52-wk high (479.00) on 6/30, RSI 62. [DATA] Q2 earnings 7/16 (est EPS 3.77, ~12 ticks out) is both the catalyst and the main risk — a strong print leads the group. [ASSUMED] Sized 0.13 (below MU/MRVL) precisely because it's at a high into a binary earnings event.
rationale.LLY: Non-cyclical diversifier. [DATA] GLP-1 franchise growth that does not co-move with semis; P/E 44.3, RSI 61, near its 6/29 52-wk high (1238). [ASSUMED] This is the book's ballast against a semis-correlated drawdown — growth exposure that isn't rate- or chip-cycle-sensitive. Carried over from last tick's target list where I'd flagged adding it.
rationale.BAC: The one cyclical kept. [DATA] Cheapest finalist at 14.4x with a 1.9% yield and RSI 68; financials are the explicit beneficiary of the de-escalation/'improved economic growth' rotation, and Q2 earnings land 7/14 (est EPS 1.10). [ASSUMED] I cut JPM and GE but keep BAC small (0.08) as a single value/financial diversifier so the book isn't purely semis+pharma. I already hold it (-1.6%).

reflection.looking_back: On 6/29 I set targets MU 0.11 / BAC 0.10 / JPM 0.10 / TSM 0.08 / GE 0.07 / LLY 0.07 (~53% invested, ~47% cash) and began rotating out of the UNH defensive hedge toward semis. The book returned +0.2% while SPY ran +1.6% — a -1.4pt alpha miss. All-time the book is -10.3% vs SPY -1.3% (-9.0pts behind), annualized Sharpe -4.74 over 12 noisy sessions.
worked: Holding the MU core paid: it's +1.5% vs entry and the cheapest semis P/E (25.9); refusing to panic-sell the 6/26 -7.5% scare was the right call [DATA].

The instinct to rotate toward semis was directionally correct — semis are the confirmed leadership (6/29 Nasdaq-100 +2.3%; Astera +16%, KLA +12%) and my RS screen now ranks MRVL/MU/TSM at the top of the universe [DATA].
missed: Too cautious, too slow: at ~47% cash with a heavy financials/industrials value sleeve, the book made only +0.2% on a +1.6% SPY day — I under-deployed into the leadership I had already identified [DATA].
The BAC/JPM/GE value tilt lagged the semis/tech that actually led the tape; carrying three correlated cyclicals diluted the semis call I was trying to make [DATA].
reflection.adjustment: Commit to the leadership instead of dabbling: exit JPM and GE, finish cutting the UNH hedge, keep only one cheap financial (BAC 0.08), and concentrate the rotation into a semis-led core (MU/MRVL/TSM ~44%) plus an LLY growth hedge — raising deployment from ~53% to ~62%, with cash (not a defensive stock) as the sole downside hedge.
